marketplace.page_subtitle: Extend QoreDB with plugins: snippet packs, themes, connection templates and WASM modules.
marketplace.section_subtitle: Every plugin in this list passed maintainer review and lives in the public registry on GitHub.
marketplace.empty: No plugins yet. Be the first to publish one.
marketplace.registry_unavailable: The registry is temporarily unreachable. Please try again in a few minutes.

detail.install_step_1: Open QoreDB → Settings → Browse and click Install. The app downloads the archive, verifies its sha256 against this page, then validates the manifest before any hook fires.
detail.install_step_2: Or download the archive directly and install it via Settings → Plugins → Install plugin. The QoreDB host re-checks the manifest the same way.
detail.manifest_no_integrity: Unsigned — QoreDB will flag this plugin as Unsigned in the panel.
detail.not_found_subtitle: This plugin is not in the registry. It may have been renamed or removed.

submit.title: Submit a plugin
submit.subtitle: Plugins are reviewed by the QoreDB maintainers before they appear in the marketplace. The review typically takes a few days — we look at the manifest, the requested capabilities, and the source of the WASM module.
submit.field_plugin_id_hint: Reverse-DNS, e.g. acme.linter. Must match plugin.json#id.
submit.field_kind_declarative: Declarative — snippets, themes, connection templates only
submit.field_kind_executable: Executable — ships a sandboxed WASM module
submit.field_contact_email_hint: We'll reach out for follow-up questions or to confirm approval. Not published.
submit.field_manifest_hint: Paste the manifest verbatim. The plugin id and version must match the fields above.
submit.field_archive_hint: Flat archive containing plugin.json and the WASM module (if any). Max 8 MiB — the same install budget QoreDB enforces.
submit.success_body: Thanks. Your plugin is in the review queue — we'll email {{email}} once it's been looked at.
checklist_items: The WASM module instantiates cleanly and exports the hooks declared in the manifest.
Every capability the manifest requests is genuinely needed — users see them in the consent dialog.
If the plugin makes HTTP calls, allowedHosts is a precise list, not a wildcard.
runtime.integrity carries the sha256 of the final .wasm bytes.
The archive contains only plugin.json + the WASM module at its root.
submit.field_category_hint: Pick the closest fit. The reviewer can adjust during review.

database.title: Overview
database.description: Global metrics and quick access to tables.

schema.title: Schema Explorer
schema.description: Visualize your relationships and database structure.

table.title: Data View
table.description: Smooth manipulation with a high-performance grid.

query.title: SQL Editor
query.description: Write and run queries with autocompletion and syntax highlighting.

blog_section.title: Latest articles
blog_section.subtitle: Insights, tutorials, and news about QoreDB and modern development.

features.title: Everything you need, <accent>nothing you don't</accent>
features.subtitle: 15 drivers, one interface. No Electron, no cloud dependency, no bloat.

unified.title: 15 Native Drivers
unified.desc: PostgreSQL family (incl. Supabase, Neon, TimescaleDB), MySQL/MariaDB, SQL Server, SQLite, DuckDB, MongoDB, Redis, CockroachDB, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch and ClickHouse.

workspaces.title: Workspaces
workspaces.desc: Group connections, queries, notebooks and history by project. Switch context in one click.

time_travel.title: Time Travel
time_travel.desc: Browse the history of any row, compare versions side by side and generate rollback SQL in one click.

vault.title: Security & Privacy
vault.desc: Encrypted credentials (Argon2), proxy support and GDPR compliant (zero telemetry).

ssh.title: SSH Tunneling
ssh.desc: Native OpenSSH client with proxy jump, ssh-agent integration and clear error reporting.

editor.title: Smart Editor
editor.desc: Syntax highlighting, autocomplete (incl. MongoDB collections), real-time linting and multi-statement execution.

library.title: Query Library
library.desc: Folders, tags, JSON import/export and reusable queries, convertible to notebooks in one click.

envs.title: Environment Protection
envs.desc: Distinct Dev/Staging/Prod, mutation blocking in prod, governance limits and audit redaction.

grid.title: High Performance Grid
grid.desc: Virtualization, advanced server-side filters, bulk edit, inline editing and infinite scroll.

ai.title: AI Assistant
ai.desc: Query generation, explanation and error correction with your own API keys (BYOK).

federation.title: Cross-DB Federation
federation.desc: Run SQL queries across multiple databases with joins between active connections.

instant_api.title: Instant Data API
instant_api.desc: Turn any saved query into a secure REST endpoint — token auth, rate limiting, and auto-generated OpenAPI docs in one click.

plugins.title: Plugin system
plugins.desc: Declarative packs (snippets, themes, templates) or sandboxed WASM modules that hook the query lifecycle.

export.title: Multi-Format Export
export.desc: Export results as CSV, JSON, SQL, HTML, XLSX, Parquet or self-contained HTML. Share via configurable providers.

opensource.title: Open Source
opensource.desc: Auditable code available on GitHub. AUR packages for Arch Linux.

sandbox.title: Sandbox Editing + Diff + Migrations
sandbox.desc: Safely edit data locally, visualize changes with cellular diff, and generate reproducible SQL scripts.

er_diagram.title: Interactive ER Diagram
er_diagram.desc: A living schema explorer. Navigate tables and relations visually, zoom, pan, and jump directly to data.

federation.title: Cross-Database Federation
federation.desc: Run one SQL query that JOINs tables across several live connections — Postgres, MySQL and more — with no CSV exports and no warehouse to stand up.

list.join: JOIN across active connections in a single query
list.nowarehouse: No exports, no ETL, no warehouse to maintain

safety_net.title: Universal Query Safety Net
safety_net.desc: Active error prevention. Detects dangerous queries (DELETE without WHERE) and provides smart alerts.

list.audit: Local audit log for sensitive operations

data_diff.title: Visual Data Diff
data_diff.desc: Compare two query results or tables side by side, like a Git diff for your data. Perfect for comparing Prod vs Staging.

list.compare: Side-by-side comparison of any two result sets
list.highlight: Cell-level highlighting of additions, deletions and changes
list.snapshot: Save result snapshots for later comparison

cta.description: Download QoreDB and see the difference in 30 seconds.

transparency.text: This roadmap reflects the current direction of the project. It is subject to evolve based on community feedback and real-world usage.

what_is_qoredb.question: What exactly is QoreDB?
what_is_qoredb.answer:
QoreDB is a **modern, open-source, local-first database client**, designed for developers who want to work efficiently with SQL and NoSQL databases, without depending on an imposed cloud.

It focuses on:

* **performance**,
* **security**,
* a **polished developer experience**,
* and a long-term vision more ambitious than traditional tools.

difference_dbeaver.question: How is QoreDB different from DBeaver or existing tools?
difference_dbeaver.answer:
QoreDB does not seek to be a "feature-by-feature" alternative.

Key differences:

* **Local-first** architecture (no data sent by default)
* Built-in security and guardrails (environments, read-only, production rules)
* User experience designed for speed and clarity
* Unified SQL + NoSQL vision
* Modern, extensible, long-term oriented codebase

QoreDB is designed as a **daily work tool**, not an "aging Swiss Army knife".

is_opensource.question: Is QoreDB really open-source?
is_opensource.answer:
Yes.

The core of QoreDB is **open-source**, readable, auditable, and modifiable.
Transparency is part of the project's DNA.

Eventually, some advanced features may exist as a paid offering, **without ever removing essential capabilities from the open-source version**.

is_free.question: Is QoreDB free?
is_free.answer:
Yes.

QoreDB is **free to download and use**, on macOS, Windows, and Linux.

A paid offer may exist in the future for advanced or professional uses, but the core of the product will remain free and usable without cost.

data_privacy.question: Is my data sent anywhere?
data_privacy.answer:
No.

By default:

* Connections, queries, and data remain **strictly local**.
* No database is proxied or stored on a remote server.

Measurement tools (analytics) are:

* **disabled by default**
* **anonymous**
* **explicit opt-in**

QoreDB adopts a **privacy-first** philosophy.

analytics.question: Do you use analytics tools?
analytics.answer:
Yes, but with strict rules.

Analytics serve only to:

* understand feature adoption,
* improve user experience,
* prioritize developments.

They **never** collect:

* database data,
* complete SQL queries,
* credentials,
* sensitive content.

Activation is always **voluntary**.

production_ready.question: Is QoreDB suitable for production use?
production_ready.answer:
Yes, with guardrails.

QoreDB integrates:

* environments (dev / staging / prod),
* a read-only mode,
* protections against dangerous queries,
* cancellation and timeout mechanisms.

The goal is to reduce **human errors**, without slowing down developers.

supported_databases.question: Which databases are supported?
supported_databases.answer:
QoreDB currently supports 15 native drivers:

* PostgreSQL
* Supabase, Neon, TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL family)
* MySQL / MariaDB
* SQL Server (incl. Windows authentication: NTLM and Kerberos)
* SQLite
* DuckDB
* MongoDB (with editor autocomplete and aggregation linting)
* Redis (with full key management)
* CockroachDB
* ClickHouse (HTTP/HTTPS, AST safety classification)
* Elasticsearch (multiple auth modes, dedicated search editor, SQL mode)
* OpenSearch (multiple auth modes, dedicated search editor, SQL mode)

More engines are planned as the architecture evolves.

remote_ssh.question: Does it work with remote connections and SSH?
remote_ssh.answer:
Yes.

QoreDB supports:

* classic remote connections,
* SSH tunnels,
* different authentication modes depending on the databases.

Connection security is a priority.

platforms.question: On which systems is QoreDB available?
platforms.answer:
QoreDB is available on:

* macOS
* Windows
* Linux

Builds are updated regularly.

maintenance.question: Is the project actively maintained?
maintenance.answer:
Yes.

QoreDB is in active development, with:

* frequent releases,
* structural refactors,
* a public roadmap,
* gradual opening to the community.

The project has already passed its POC stage.

contribute.question: Can I contribute to the project?
contribute.answer:
Yes, and it is encouraged.

Possible contributions:

* user feedback,
* GitHub issues and discussions,
* improvement proposals,
* code and documentation.

A community GitHub is being structured.

funding.question: How will the project be funded?
funding.answer:
The targeted model is:

* sustainable open-source,
* self-funded,
* without dependence on a mandatory centralized cloud.

Details will evolve with product adoption.

professional_use.question: Can I use QoreDB in a professional setting?
professional_use.answer:
Yes.

QoreDB is designed to be used:

* by solo developers,
* early startups,
* technical teams.

Security, stability, and maintenance aspects are taken seriously.

follow_progress.question: Where can I follow the project's evolution?
follow_progress.answer:
* On the official website
* Via the public roadmap
* On LinkedIn (regular sharing of project progress)
* On GitHub

join_beta.question: How to join the beta or test QoreDB?
join_beta.answer:
QoreDB is already available for download.

You can:

* install it directly,
* join the beta via the site,
* share your feedback to influence the future of the project.

long_term.question: Can I trust QoreDB in the long term?
long_term.answer:
QoreDB is designed as a **long-term project**, not an experiment.

Technical, product, and open-source choices go in the direction of:

* sustainability,
* transparency,
* developer trust.

pricing_page.subtitle: Free for solo work, Pro for the daily job.

core.title: Free
core.description: The complete open-source foundation of QoreDB.

features.drivers: 15 native drivers (PostgreSQL family with Supabase, Neon, TimescaleDB; MySQL/MariaDB, SQL Server, SQLite, DuckDB, MongoDB, Redis, CockroachDB, ClickHouse, Elasticsearch, OpenSearch)
features.crud: Full CRUD + SQL/Mongo editor with autocomplete and linting
features.workspaces: Workspaces:group connections, queries and notebooks by project
features.grid: High-performance data grid with bulk edit and advanced filters
features.er_diagram: Interactive ER diagram and Table Insights
features.ddl: DDL management: CREATE / ALTER TABLE, foreign keys, indexes
features.history: Query history, favorites and tab groups
features.vault: Encrypted credential vault (Argon2) + proxy and ssh-agent support
features.safety: Production safety rules (env labels, read-only mode) + basic audit log with redaction
features.audit_basic: Basic audit log (10,000 entries) with query redaction
features.export_basic: CSV / JSON / SQL export and shareable result links
features.shortcuts: Query history, favorites, tab groups, keyboard shortcuts and 9 UI languages

pro.title: Pro
pro.description: The natural upgrade for demanding developers.

features.time_travel: Time Travel:row history, version diff and rollback SQL

team.title: Team
team.description: Pro features for the whole team, managed per seat.
team.nominative_note: Named seats: one license per person.
team.page_subtitle: Pro features for the whole team — named seats, central billing, priority support.

open_core_why.question: Why an Open Core model?
open_core_why.answer: To make the solo project sustainable long term. Core stays free forever. Pro funds continuous development.

data_sent.question: Is any of my data sent somewhere?
data_sent.answer: No. QoreDB is 100% local-first. Your data never leaves your machine. Even AI runs with your own API keys.

lifetime_updates.question: Are updates included for life?
lifetime_updates.answer: Yes. One payment, lifetime updates included — no subscription, no renewal, no paywall on future versions.

try_pro.question: Can I try Pro features first?
try_pro.answer: Core includes a limited preview of selected Pro features (for example: 3 free Sandbox changes) so you can evaluate before buying.

pro_source_code.question: Is Pro source code available?
pro_source_code.answer: Pro features are under BSL 1.1 and automatically convert to Apache 2.0 after 4 years. Code is readable but redistribution is restricted during that period.

team_sharing.question: Can I share one Team key with several people?
team_sharing.answer: No. The Team licence is per seat: each member receives their own named key (one key per person). Sharing a key or using the software beyond the purchased seats is prohibited by the terms of use.

intro.title: 1. Data Controller
intro.content:
The data controller is the editor of the QoreDB project.

Complete information regarding the legal entity will be specified as soon as the legal structure is finalized.

data_collection.title: 2. Data Collection
data_collection.content:
QoreDB strictly limits the collection of personal data.

2.1 Navigation Data (Website)

When consulting the site, anonymized technical data may be collected for statistical purposes, including:

  • pages visited,
  • device type and browser,
  • operating system,
  • approximate country or geographic area,
  • load times and performance.

This data does not allow for the personal identification of a user.

QoreDB uses Vercel Analytics, a privacy-respecting analytics solution:

  • no cookies,
  • no cross-site tracking,
  • no advertising profiling.

2.2 Voluntarily Provided Data

Certain data may be voluntarily provided by the user, for example:

  • during contact,
  • during registration for a newsletter,
  • during the use of future services requiring authentication.

Collected data may include:

  • email address,
  • name or pseudonym,
  • message content.

purposes.title: 3. Purposes of Processing
purposes.content:
Collected data is used solely to:

  • ensure the proper functioning of the site and services,
  • improve performance and user experience,
  • respond to contact requests,
  • communicate information related to the QoreDB project.

No personal data is sold, rented, or ceded to third parties for commercial purposes.

legal_basis.title: 4. Legal Basis for Processing
legal_basis.content:
Personal data processing is based on:

  • QoreDB's legitimate interest (anonymous statistics, security),
  • the user's explicit consent when required,
  • the execution of a request initiated by the user.

cookies.title: 5. Cookies and Trackers
cookies.content:
QoreDB does not use advertising or behavioral tracking cookies.

The analytical tools used do not require prior consent in accordance with applicable regulations.

Should cookies be used in the future, this policy would be updated and a consent mechanism would be implemented.

hosting.title: 6. Hosting and Sub-processors
hosting.content:
The QoreDB site and services are hosted by providers respecting high standards of security and compliance.

Data may be processed on servers located in the European Union or in countries offering an adequate level of protection in accordance with the GDPR.

security.title: 7. Data Security
security.content:
QoreDB implements appropriate technical and organizational measures to guarantee the security, integrity, and confidentiality of data, including:

  • encryption of communications,
  • access control,
  • monitoring and limitation of unauthorized access.

retention.title: 8. Retention Period
retention.content:
Personal data is retained only for the duration necessary for the purposes for which it was collected.

Anonymized statistical data may be kept without time limitation.

rights.title: 9. User Rights
rights.content:
In accordance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), users have the following rights:

  • right of access,
  • right of rectification,
  • right to erasure,
  • right to restriction of processing,
  • right to object,
  • right to data portability.

Any request can be made via the contact means indicated on the site.

third_party.title: 10. Links to Third-Party Services
third_party.content: The QoreDB site may contain links to third-party services (e.g., GitHub). QoreDB is not responsible for the privacy policies applied by these services.

changes.title: 11. Policy Changes
changes.content:
QoreDB reserves the right to modify this Privacy Policy at any time.

Users are invited to consult it regularly. The date of the last update is indicated at the top of the page.

contact.title: 12. Contact
contact.content: For any questions regarding personal data protection, you can contact the QoreDB team via the official channels indicated on the site.

editor.title: Site Editor
editor.content:
**Project Name / Product:** QoreDB
**Publication Director:** Raphaël Plassart
**Status:** Software Project (open-core)

> Information regarding the legal entity (company name, legal form, address, registration number) will be specified as soon as the legal structure is finalized.

hosting.title: Hosting
hosting.content:
The QoreDB website is hosted by **Vercel Inc.**, located at 340 S Lemon Ave #4133 Walnut, CA 91789, USA.

Data may be hosted on infrastructures located within the European Union or in countries offering a level of data protection compliant with current regulations.

ip.title: Intellectual Property
ip.content:
All content present on the QoreDB site, including in particular:

* texts,
* documentation,
* blog posts,
* graphic elements,
* visual identity,
* the QoreDB name, logo, and brand,

is protected by copyright and laws relating to intellectual property.

Unless otherwise stated, QoreDB source code is distributed under an **open-core** model:

* a part is published under an open-source license,
* certain advanced features may be subject to a proprietary license.

Any unauthorized reproduction, representation, modification, or exploitation of the protected content or elements is strictly prohibited.

liability.title: Site Access and Liability
liability.content:
QoreDB makes every effort to ensure the accuracy of the information disseminated on the site. However, the project cannot be held responsible for:

* errors or omissions,
* temporary unavailability of the site,
* direct or indirect damages related to the use of the site or software.

Use of QoreDB is under the sole responsibility of the user.

external_links.title: External Links
external_links.content: The QoreDB site may contain links to third-party sites. QoreDB exercises no control over these sites and declines all responsibility regarding their content or practices.

privacy.title: Personal Data
privacy.content:
Personal data management is detailed in the dedicated page:

➡️ [Privacy Policy](/privacy)

QoreDB undertakes to comply with applicable regulations, in particular the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.content:
Use of the QoreDB site, documentation, and software implies full acceptance of the:

➡️ [Terms of Use](#)

license.title: License
license.content:
The license conditions applicable to the QoreDB software are detailed in the dedicated page:

➡️ [License](#)

contact.title: Contact
contact.content: For any legal question, report, or request for information, you can contact the QoreDB team via the official channels indicated on the site.

terms_page.title: Terms of Service
terms_page.intro:
These Terms of Service (hereinafter "ToS") govern the access to and use of the **QoreDB** website, its documentation, its online services, and the QoreDB software.

By accessing the site or using the QoreDB software, the user acknowledges having read these ToS and accepting them without reservation.

definitions.title: 1. Definitions
definitions.content:
* **QoreDB**: refers to the software project, the website, the documentation, the associated tools and services.
* **User**: any person accessing the site or using the QoreDB software.
* **Software**: the QoreDB software, distributed under an open-core model.
* **Site**: the official QoreDB website.

object.title: 2. Object
object.content: The purpose of these ToS is to define the conditions under which users can access the QoreDB site, consult the documentation, use the resources made available, and operate the QoreDB software.

access.title: 3. Access to site and services
access.content:
Access to the QoreDB site is free of charge, unless otherwise indicated.

QoreDB reserves the right to:

* suspend, restrict, or interrupt access to the site or any service,
* modify the content or features offered,
* perform maintenance operations.

No guarantee is given regarding the continuous availability of the site or services.

software_use.title: 4. Use of QoreDB Software
software_use.content:
The QoreDB software is distributed under an **open-core** model:

* a part of the code is published under an open-source license,
* certain features, extensions, or services may be subject to a separate proprietary license.

The precise conditions for use, modification, and distribution of the software are defined in the **QoreDB License**, available on the site and in the official repositories.

user_obligations.title: 6. User Obligations
user_obligations.content:
The user agrees to:

* use QoreDB in accordance with applicable laws and regulations,
* not infringe on the security, integrity, or proper functioning of the site or the software,
* not attempt unauthorized access to systems or data,
* not use QoreDB for fraudulent, illegal, or abusive purposes.

Any abusive use may result in the suspension of access to services.

ip.title: 7. Intellectual Property
ip.content:
All elements of the QoreDB site (texts, documentation, graphics, logo, visual identity) are protected by intellectual property rights.

Unless otherwise stated, any unauthorized reproduction, representation, or exploitation is prohibited.

The source code of the QoreDB software is subject to the terms of the applicable license, as defined in the **License** page.

liability.title: 8. Liability
liability.content:
QoreDB is provided "as is", without warranty of any kind.

QoreDB cannot be held liable for:

* direct or indirect damages resulting from the use of the site or software,
* data loss, business interruption, or commercial damages,
* consequences related to improper configuration or use of the software.

The user is solely responsible for their use of QoreDB and the data they process.

privacy.title: 9. Personal Data
privacy.content: Personal data processing is governed by the **Privacy Policy** accessible on the QoreDB site.

external_links.title: 10. External Links
external_links.content: The QoreDB site may contain links to third-party sites or services. QoreDB exercises no control over these services and declines all responsibility regarding their content or functioning.

updates.title: 11. Changes to ToS
updates.content:
QoreDB reserves the right to modify these ToS at any time.

Users are invited to regularly consult the current version. The date of the last update is indicated at the top of the page.

law.title: 12. Applicable Law
law.content:
These ToS are governed by French law.

In the event of a dispute, and failing amicable resolution, the competent courts will be those of the applicable jurisdiction in accordance with the legislation in force.

contact.title: 13. Contact
contact.content: For any question relating to these Terms of Service, the user can contact the QoreDB team via the official channels indicated on the site.

team_licensing.title: 5. Team licences (named seats)
team_licensing.content:
The **Team** licence is granted **per seat**. It grants as many **named seats** as the number of seats purchased, at the rate of **one (1) licence key per person**.

* each seat key is tied to its holder's email address,
* **sharing a key** among several people is **prohibited**,
* using the software **beyond the number of purchased seats** is **prohibited**.

The subscription holder (administrator) assigns the seats and ensures compliance with these terms within their organisation. Any breach may result in the suspension or termination of the licence.

quick_start_page.title: Quick Start
quick_start_page.description: Browse, query, and manage your data in a few clicks.

install.title: 1. Install and Launch
install.content: Download the latest version from the project's Releases page, install the application, and open it.

connect.title: 2. Create Your First Connection
connect.content:
Click on + in the sidebar to add a database. QoreDB ships 15 native drivers:

  • PostgreSQL · Supabase · Neon · TimescaleDB
  • MySQL / MariaDB
  • SQL Server (Windows auth supported)
  • SQLite · DuckDB
  • MongoDB · Redis · CockroachDB
  • ClickHouse · Elasticsearch · OpenSearch

Paste a connection string for instant driver detection, or enter host, port, database, user and environment (dev/staging/prod). Group everything into Workspaces to keep projects clean.

explore.title: 3. Explore Your Data
explore.content:
Once connected:

  • Navigate through schemas and tables
  • Open a table to visualize data
  • Run SQL queries in the integrated editor

daily.title: 4. Save Time Every Day
daily.content:
• History & favorites to find your queries
  • Global search to move fast
  • Read-only mode to secure production
  • Light/Dark theme and multi-tab interface

security_tip.title: Security Tip
security_tip.content: QoreDB stores your credentials securely via the system's native keychain and supports SSH tunneling.

links_page.title: All QoreDB links
links_page.subtitle: Find every official link in one place.

site.title: Website
site.description: The product story in one place.

github.title: GitHub
github.description: Browse the code, issues, and updates.

discord.title: Discord
discord.description: Join the community and share feedback.

inline_cta.text: Ready to try? Download QoreDB for free.

pricing_preview.title: Simple, transparent pricing
pricing_preview.subtitle: Core is free forever. Pro unlocks power-user features.
pricing_preview.core_2: Workspaces, DDL, bulk edit and advanced filters
pricing_preview.core_4: Production safety guards and 9 UI languages
pricing_preview.pro_price_note: Everything in Core, plus advanced workflows.

metadata.site_description: A fast, secure modern database client for developers. Unified SQL + NoSQL, secure vault, local-first.
metadata.blog_description: Articles, updates and technical deep dives from the QoreDB team.
metadata.changelog_description: Stay up to date with the latest changes and improvements to QoreDB.

blog_page.title: The QoreDB Blog
blog_page.subtitle: Insights, tutorials, and news about QoreDB and modern development.
blog_page.try_adjusting: Try adjusting your filters or search term.

docs.not_found_body: This documentation page does not exist or hasn't been written yet.
docs.landing_subtitle: Everything you need to install QoreDB, connect your databases, and ship faster.
docs.fallback_banner_body: This page hasn't been translated yet. The English version is shown below.

features_common.not_found_subtitle: This feature page does not exist.

features_index.title: Flagship features
features_index.subtitle: What QoreDB does beyond a classic SQL client. For each feature: the mechanism, an example, its limits.

federation.title: Cross-database federation
federation.subtitle: Run one SQL query that JOINs tables across several databases — no CSV exports, no warehouse to stand up.
federation.teaser: A single SQL query to join tables from several active connections — PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB and the rest.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: Your data lives across several databases: PostgreSQL for the app, MySQL for billing, MongoDB for events. Answering one question that spans them means exporting CSVs, writing glue scripts, or standing up a warehouse. For a one-off check or a reconciliation between prod and billing, that is out of proportion.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: QoreDB detects table references that cross several connections and runs the join locally, in an in-memory, ephemeral DuckDB engine. You write standard SQL; the engine handles the rest.

steps.title: Reference the table by its full path
steps.body: A table from an active connection is named as connection.database.table (or connection.database.schema.table). The connection alias is the normalized name of your saved connection.

steps.title: QoreDB plans the fetch
steps.body: Only the columns you use are read, and WHERE filters are pushed down to each source where possible, to keep the transferred volume small.

steps.title: The join runs in memory
steps.body: Rows from each source are loaded into an in-memory DuckDB engine, then the query runs there. Nothing is persisted: the instance is destroyed once the query completes.

steps.title: You get a single result
steps.body: The result comes back like any query, with per-source detail (rows read, duration) and any warnings.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: Here, prod_pg is a PostgreSQL connection and billing_mysql a MySQL connection. One query joins the app's users with the billing orders:
example.caption: Two databases, two engines, one join — with no intermediate export. The query stays read-only: it never writes to your sources.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: Federation targets operational joins and reconciliation, not replacing an analytical warehouse. Its boundaries are explicit:
items: Each source is capped at 100,000 rows by default (configurable per query); a warning is shown when the cap is hit.
Rows are materialized in memory in DuckDB: federation targets reasonable volumes, not unbounded analytical scans over very large tables.
A 30 s timeout per source, and a 60 s default timeout for the whole query.
SELECT queries only, a single statement: a federated query never writes to the underlying databases.
At least two active connections are required to build a cross-database join.

cta.title: Join your databases without a pipeline
cta.body: Cross-database federation is part of QoreDB Pro. Download the app and try it on your own connections.

time-travel.title: Data Time-Travel
time-travel.subtitle: A “git blame” for your data: who changed what, when, and the SQL to undo it.
time-travel.teaser: Row-by-row history of the mutations made in QoreDB, with a temporal diff and rollback SQL.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: A value is wrong in the database. Who changed it, when, and what was the previous value? Without a log, all you have is the current state. One UPDATE without a WHERE, one mistyped manual fix, and you are restoring a whole dump to recover a handful of rows.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: When you change data from QoreDB, the app records a before/after image of each affected row in a local log. You get a timeline, the history of a specific row, a diff between two points in time, and the SQL to undo.

steps.title: Before/after capture
steps.body: On every INSERT, UPDATE or DELETE performed in QoreDB, the app records the operation, the primary key, the changed columns, and the row state before and after.

steps.title: Timeline and per-row history
steps.body: The timeline groups changes together; the history panel shows, for a given row, all of its successive versions — a blame at the data level.

steps.title: Temporal diff
steps.body: Compare a table's state between two points in time, T1 and T2: rows added, modified, removed, with the changed columns spelled out.

steps.title: Rollback SQL
steps.body: QoreDB generates the SQL that reverses a selection of changes (INSERT → DELETE, UPDATE → previous value, DELETE → re-insert), in reverse chronological order and wrapped in BEGIN/COMMIT. You review it in the editor before running it.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: After an overly broad UPDATE, QoreDB produces the undo SQL, with literal values to review:
example.caption: The rollback is never applied automatically: it is generated for you to review and run yourself. Changes that cannot be fully reversed are flagged.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: Time-Travel is an application-level log, not a database-engine capability. Its boundaries:
items: Only mutations made from QoreDB are captured. External writes — your application, psql, another tool — are not.
The row must have a primary key: it is what identifies the before/after image and makes rollback possible.
The “before” image is captured on a best-effort basis (a 2 s ceiling so it can't slow the mutation down); if it fails, the reversal may be partial and a warning says so.
The log is local, with configurable rotation and retention; sensitive columns can be redacted ([REDACTED]) before being written to disk.
The rollback SQL is generated for review, never executed automatically.

cta.title: Keep track of every change
cta.body: Data Time-Travel is part of QoreDB Pro. Download the app and enable capture on your own connections.

data-contracts.title: Data Contracts
data-contracts.subtitle: Declarative data-quality assertions, written in YAML and executed as SQL against your tables.
data-contracts.teaser: 12 rule types (presence, format, domain, uniqueness, referential, cardinality, custom SQL) in a versionable YAML contract.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: Data quality erodes silently: a column filling up with NULLs, a status outside the expected set, an orphaned foreign key. You notice when a report is wrong. Writing check queries by hand every time, then remembering to re-run them, doesn't hold up over time.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: You describe your expectations in a YAML contract — a table, a list of rules. QoreDB translates each rule into engine-specific SQL, runs it, and aggregates the results into a health status. The contract lives in your repository, versioned with the rest.

steps.title: A declarative contract
steps.body: A YAML file targets a table and lists typed rules. Each rule carries an id, a severity (error, warning, info) and its parameters. The file is stored in .qoredb/contracts/, so it's versioned alongside your code.

steps.title: 12 rule types
steps.body: Presence (not_null_pct, not_empty), format (regex_match, length_range), domain (numeric_range, date_range, allowed_values), uniqueness (unique, distinct_count), referential integrity (foreign_key_integrity), volume (row_count) and custom SQL.

steps.title: Executed as SQL, multi-engine
steps.body: The runner generates dialect-specific SQL and runs it through the active connection. Compatible with PostgreSQL and its derivatives, MySQL/MariaDB, SQLite, DuckDB, SQL Server and ClickHouse.

steps.title: Health status and samples
steps.body: Each run produces a per-rule status (passed / failed) and a contract health badge. For failing rules, QoreDB collects a sample of the offending rows (configurable limit). A dedicated notebook cell lets you run a contract inside a report.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: A contract for an orders table: email present, status within a closed domain, foreign-key integrity, positive total.
example.caption: The contract is a versioned file. It detects violations when it runs — it doesn't replace database constraints, it complements them where they're missing.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: Contracts are a detection tool, not a blocking mechanism. Their boundaries:
items: SQL engines only: the runner relies on SQL execution (PostgreSQL and derivatives, MySQL/MariaDB, SQLite, DuckDB, SQL Server, ClickHouse). NoSQL databases are not covered.
Rules are evaluated when the contract is run (manually or via the notebook cell): they detect issues, they don't prevent bad writes the way a SQL constraint would.
The custom_sql rule is only as reliable as the SQL you write in it: its correctness is on you.
On large tables, the collection of offending-row samples is capped (configurable limit) and can be turned off.

cta.title: Formalize your expectations on the data
cta.body: Data Contracts are part of QoreDB Pro. Download the app and write your first contract.

instant-api.title: Instant Data API
instant-api.subtitle: Turn a parameterized SQL query into a read-only REST endpoint on your machine, in about a minute.
instant-api.teaser: A read-only GET endpoint on 127.0.0.1, authenticated with a Bearer token, documented by an OpenAPI 3.1 file generated from your live endpoints.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: A colleague needs a number that lives in your database. A script needs to read one table. A local dashboard needs one query. Each time the answer is the same: stand up a small backend, wire a connection, expose a route, handle authentication. Hours of plumbing for a query you had already written.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: QoreDB embeds an HTTP server that serves your queries as GET endpoints. You declare the query and its parameters; QoreDB handles routing, typing, authentication and documentation. The server binds to 127.0.0.1 and nothing else — there is no setting to expose it on the network.

steps.title: Declare the endpoint
steps.body: You give it a name, pick a connection, and write the SQL query with its parameters marked as {{country}}. Each parameter is declared with a type (string, integer, float, boolean), a required flag and an optional default.

steps.title: Start the server
steps.body: It listens on 127.0.0.1:4787. HTTPS is available through a self-signed certificate generated in memory at startup and never written to disk. Starting is a deliberate action: this is not a background service that runs behind your back.

steps.title: Call it with a token
steps.body: Each endpoint carries its own Bearer token, shown once at creation. Only an Argon2id hash is stored on disk, never the token itself. Regenerating a token takes one click and revokes the previous one immediately.

steps.title: Read the OpenAPI document
steps.body: GET /openapi.json returns an OpenAPI 3.1 document generated from the live registry: one path per endpoint, typed parameters and the bearer security scheme. Feed it to your client generator.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: An endpoint named orders_top, on a PostgreSQL connection, with one required string parameter:
example.caption: Values are never concatenated raw: each parameter is escaped for the target dialect, and the final SQL is parsed again before execution. Anything classified as a mutation is rejected.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: The Instant Data API targets local scripts, notebooks and one-off integrations. It is not an application backend, and its boundaries are explicit:
items: Loopback only. The server binds to 127.0.0.1; there is no option to expose it on the network.
No CORS headers, so a web page cannot call these endpoints from a browser. The targets are curl, scripts, local backends and notebooks.
GET only, read-only. The final SQL is analyzed on every request and rejected if it is classified as a mutation. That check runs at request time, not when you save the endpoint.
Rate limit of 10 requests per second per endpoint, burst of 10, not configurable.
The row cap (100 by default) trims the response after the query has already run. For a large table, write your own LIMIT in the query.
No pagination and no query timeout: a slow query blocks until the driver returns.
Fourteen driver IDs are supported — the PostgreSQL family, MySQL/MariaDB, SQLite, DuckDB, SQL Server and ClickHouse. MongoDB, Redis and the others are rejected.
The server does not start on its own; you start it each session. Your endpoint definitions do persist.
An endpoint cannot be edited. Changing a query means deleting and recreating it, which issues a new token.

cta.title: Expose a query, not a backend
cta.body: The Instant Data API is part of QoreDB Pro. Download the app and turn your first query into an endpoint.

sandbox.title: Sandbox mode
sandbox.subtitle: Hold your grid edits locally, read the SQL they produce, then apply them — or drop them and move on.
sandbox.teaser: Edits made in the data grid are held locally instead of being written. You read the SQL before anything reaches the database.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: In a data grid, an edit is a write. You change a cell and it is already committed. On a production table, a mistyped value or a delete on the wrong row leaves no window to reconsider. And when ten changes belong together, there is no moment where you see them as a set, before they land.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: Sandbox mode intercepts the edits you make through the grid. Nothing is sent to the database: changes are held locally, the grid shows you the projected result, and a panel lists everything pending. When you are ready, QoreDB shows you the SQL and you decide.

steps.title: Turn it on
steps.body: One button, or Cmd/Ctrl+Shift+S. A colored border around the workspace and a badge make the state impossible to miss, because a sandbox you forgot you were in is worse than no sandbox at all.

steps.title: Edit as usual
steps.body: Inline grid editing, the row modal, deletes and bulk edit all feed the sandbox instead of the database. The grid renders the result you would get, so you can keep working on top of your own pending changes.

steps.title: Review the whole set
steps.body: A Pending Changes panel groups everything by table with per-operation counts. Redundant edits are folded together: five edits on the same new row are one insert, and deleting a row you just inserted removes it from the set entirely.

steps.title: Read the SQL, then decide
steps.body: Generate SQL opens the statements matching your changes, ready to copy or download. Apply writes them; on a production connection you have to type APPLY to confirm. Or you discard everything, and nothing ever happened.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: After editing one row and deleting another in a PostgreSQL table, the generated script reads:
example.caption: This script is what you review and can export. Applying replays each change through the driver rather than running the script itself, so what you read and what runs are equivalent, not identical.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: Sandbox mode covers the edits you make through the grid. That is a real boundary, and it is worth being precise about where it stops:
items: It covers data, not schema. CREATE, ALTER and DROP run against the database immediately, sandbox or not.
It covers grid edits, not the SQL editor and not the data generator. A DELETE typed in the editor, or a batch of generated rows, executes right away: the sandbox is not a query interceptor.
Nothing is reserved in the database. If someone changes a row while you hold it in the sandbox, applying overwrites their change.
Pending changes are stored locally by QoreDB and survive a restart. They are not a database transaction and they hold no lock.
SQL generation targets PostgreSQL, MySQL/MariaDB, SQLite and SQL Server. PostgreSQL-compatible engines fall back to PostgreSQL syntax with a warning.
Whether the apply runs inside a single transaction depends on the driver. Where it does not, a failure halfway leaves the earlier changes applied.
Updates and deletes need the primary key columns present in the result to be tracked.

cta.title: Read before you write
cta.body: Sandbox mode is part of QoreDB Pro. Download the app and put a step between your edits and your production tables.

migrations.title: Migrations Manager
migrations.subtitle: Versioned .sql migrations in your repository, applied and rolled back from the client, with the applied state tracked in the database itself.
migrations.teaser: Plain .sql files with up and down sections, committed alongside your code, and a history table that tells you what actually ran.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: Schema changes travel badly. A migration applied on a laptop and forgotten on staging. An ALTER run by hand in a console, with no trace. A rollback nobody wrote. Either you bring in a full migration framework and its runtime, or you keep a folder of SQL files and a spreadsheet, and hope.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: QoreDB reads plain .sql files from a folder in your project, applies them to the connection you choose, and records what it did in a table inside the database itself. No agent, no runtime dependency in your application: the files are Git artifacts, the state lives where the schema lives.

steps.title: One file per migration
steps.body: Files live in .qoredb/migrations/, named version_slug.sql, with a -- migrate:up section and a -- migrate:down section. Plain SQL, versioned with your code, readable without QoreDB installed.

steps.title: State in the database
steps.body: A qoredb_migrations table records the version, the checksum of the up script, when it ran, how long it took and through which connection. It is created outside the transaction, so it survives the rollback of the migration it is tracking.

steps.title: Every statement is vetted before the first one runs
steps.body: The script is split into statements while preserving the exact text, and each one is classified and checked against your safety rules before execution begins. On a production connection, dangerous statements are held back by policy rather than discovered halfway through.

steps.title: States that do not lie
steps.body: Applied, rolled back, failed. A migration that broke halfway is marked failed, not pending, because pending would suggest nothing happened. And if you edit an up script that is already applied, QoreDB refuses to re-run it: the checksum no longer matches the schema it described. Writing a new migration is the only safe repair.

steps.title: Generate the diff (Pro)
steps.body: Capture a baseline of your schema and QoreDB writes the migration for the difference: added and modified tables in the up section, the inverse in the down, with irreversible statements commented out rather than guessed. Check drift compares the live schema against your baseline, and a Prod↔Staging view diffs two connections of the same driver side by side.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: A migration adding a column and its index. This is the whole file:
example.caption: One file, two directions, no framework. It reads the same in a code review as it does in QoreDB.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: The Migrations Manager is a runner for SQL files, not a migration framework. What it does not do matters as much as what it does:
items: Eleven drivers: the PostgreSQL family, MySQL/MariaDB, SQLite, DuckDB/MotherDuck and SQL Server. MongoDB, Redis, Elasticsearch and ClickHouse are out of scope.

On MySQL and MariaDB, DDL is not transactional. Any migration containing DDL runs outside a transaction: if it fails halfway, nothing is undone and the migration is marked failed for you to inspect. MySQL also requires an explicit target database.
There is no ordering guarantee and no apply-all button. Each migration is applied on its own; nothing stops you from running the third before the first.
The rollback is the SQL you wrote in the down section. When the schema diff cannot invert a statement without inventing data, it comments it out rather than guessing.
Schema diff covers tables, columns, indexes, single-column foreign keys and primary keys. Views, sequences, triggers, procedures, CHECK constraints and composite foreign keys are not introspected.
Drift detection is a comparison you trigger, against a baseline you captured. It is not continuous monitoring, and the baseline stays on your machine: it is deliberately not committed.
Git conflicts are not handled. Two branches creating the same version are caught when you apply, not when you merge.

cta.title: Ship your schema like your code
cta.body: The Migrations Manager ships in the free QoreDB core. Schema diff generation, drift detection and Prod↔Staging comparison are Pro.

notebooks.title: Database notebooks
notebooks.subtitle: Executable documents mixing SQL and Markdown against a live connection, saved as a plain file your team can review in a pull request.
notebooks.teaser: SQL and Markdown cells, typed parameters, run all or run from here — in a JSON file that diffs cleanly in Git.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: A query that answers a real question is never just a query. It needs the context around it: why this filter, what the number actually means, what to check next. That context ends up in a Slack thread, a wiki page, or nowhere. Six months later the query is still in someone's editor and no one remembers what it was for. The tools that solve this properly — Jupyter, hosted notebooks — mean a Python runtime, another driver to configure, and your credentials in a second place.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: QoreDB notebooks are documents made of cells: SQL against your active connection, Markdown for everything the SQL cannot say. They run in the client you already have connected, and they save to a file you commit next to your code.

steps.title: Cells against a live connection
steps.body: A SQL cell runs on the notebook's connection and namespace and renders its result inline. A Markdown cell holds the reasoning. Every database QoreDB supports works, because the cell hands its source to the session unchanged — on a MongoDB connection you write MQL in the same cell and the driver interprets it.

steps.title: Parameters, typed and declared once
steps.body: Declare a variable and use it as $customer_id or {{date_from}} anywhere in the notebook. Four types — text, number, date, select — each with an input rendered for it. Change the value at the top, rerun, and the whole document follows.

steps.title: Run all, or run from here
steps.body: Execution is sequential and stops at the first error, so a broken cell does not cascade into ten misleading results below it. A run can be cancelled mid-flight, and the cancellation reaches the database rather than just the interface.

steps.title: A file, not a document in someone's cloud
steps.body: The .qnb format is indented JSON. Results and execution state are stripped on save, so a diff shows what you changed rather than what you last ran. It reviews like code because it is a file in your repository.

steps.title: AI cells (Pro)
steps.body: Describe what you want in plain language; the cell writes SQL into a new cell directly below, which you read before you run. Bring your own key — OpenAI, Anthropic, Mistral, Gemini, DeepSeek, or Ollama for a model that never leaves your machine. Keys live in your OS keychain.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: A SQL cell using two variables declared at the top of the notebook:
example.caption: Variables are substituted as quoted literals, not bound parameters. Numbers must parse finite, dates must match an ISO shape, and text is escaped before it reaches the query. They stand in for values, not identifiers: a variable in a FROM clause will not do what you want.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: A notebook is a document, not a scheduler or a BI tool. Where it stops:
items: Cells are SQL and Markdown, plus AI and contract cells on Pro. Chart cells and cross-cell references exist in the file format but have no interface yet, so treat them as unavailable.
Variables substitute values, not identifiers or SQL fragments. A variable in a FROM clause or a column position will not work.
Execution is sequential and stops at the first error. There is no continue-on-error toggle, no parallel run and no scheduling.
Results are held in memory. A SELECT with no LIMIT loads every row into the app before the cell truncates what it displays.
The notebook sets no query timeout of its own; it inherits whatever your governance policy defines, and defines none if the policy does not.
Export to Markdown keeps SQL and Markdown cells. The standalone HTML export renders Markdown as plain text rather than formatting it, and neither export includes results.
A local draft is autosaved every 30 seconds. It is a crash net, not a version history: the file on disk is the source of truth.

AI cells need a key you provide, and the schema of the tables in scope is sent to the provider you pick, with sensitive column names redacted. Your rows are not sent. Only Ollama keeps everything local.

cta.title: A query worth keeping is worth explaining
cta.body: Notebooks ship in the free QoreDB core. AI cells and contract cells are Pro.

production-safety.title: Production guardrails
production-safety.subtitle: Tag a connection as production and QoreDB reads every statement before it runs, using a real SQL parser rather than a pattern match.
production-safety.teaser: Dev, staging and production connections behave differently. Dangerous statements are classified from the parsed AST, not a regex on the first word.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: Every developer with production access has the same story: the wrong tab, a DELETE that was meant for a local database, a WHERE clause that never made it into the query. The tooling did exactly what it was told, instantly, and there was no moment in between. Most clients treat a production connection exactly like a scratch database, because to them it is just another host and port.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: A connection carries an environment: development, staging or production. On a production connection, QoreDB parses your statement before sending it, classifies what it actually does, and applies the rules you configured. The parser is the point: a client that greps for DROP misses half of what matters and flags things that do not.

steps.title: Classification from the parsed statement
steps.body: Your SQL is parsed with the dialect of the driver you are connected to, and the classification comes from the resulting syntax tree. DROP, TRUNCATE, ALTER in its many forms, an UPDATE with no WHERE, a DELETE with no WHERE, and EXPLAIN ANALYZE wrapping any of those. A data-modifying CTE — WITH x AS (DELETE ...) SELECT — is caught as a mutation, which a pattern match on the first keyword never sees.

steps.title: Fail closed in production
steps.body: If the parser cannot read your statement on a production connection, the statement does not run. QoreDB declines to guess about a query it did not understand, on the database where guessing is least affordable.

steps.title: Rules you can read and change
steps.body: Built-in rules block DROP and TRUNCATE on production, ask for confirmation on DELETE, and warn on ALTER. Each one names itself when it fires, so a refusal tells you which rule refused and why. On Pro you add your own: an environment, an operation, an optional pattern, and one of three actions — block, warn, or ask.

steps.title: Engine-specific hardening
steps.body: The risky statements are not the same on every engine. On DuckDB, INSTALL and LOAD and ATTACH and COPY TO are flagged because they reach the network or the filesystem. On SQLite, ATTACH and the PRAGMAs that turn off foreign keys or journalling are flagged. On ClickHouse, anything the classifier cannot place is treated as a mutation rather than waved through.

steps.title: A trail of what happened
steps.body: Each query is recorded with its outcome, its duration, the rule that blocked it if any, and a SHA-256 fingerprint of its normalized shape so repeated queries group together. Literals are redacted before anything is written. The log exports to JSONL or CSV.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: A DROP TABLE on a connection tagged production, with the default rules:
example.caption: The statement never reaches the database. The refusal names the rule, because a guardrail that blocks without saying which one is a guardrail you will disable.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: This is an accident guardrail, not a security control. The distinction matters, so here it is in full:
items: Nothing is enforced database-side, apart from DuckDB where read-only is set on the engine itself. Read-only and every rule are decisions QoreDB makes before sending the query. Your credentials still have exactly the rights they always had, and any other client using them is unaffected.
If a guardrail is what you need, the durable version is a database role with the rights you want. QoreDB protects you from your own slip; it does not protect a database from its users.

Out of the box, production asks for confirmation on dangerous statements but does not hard-block them by policy. Blocking is a setting, and it is off by default. Built-in rules do block DROP and TRUNCATE, and those rules can be disabled.

The confirmation is driven by the client. The backend trusts the acknowledgement it is sent, so confirmation is a guard against haste, not against a determined bypass. Only the policy-level block is unconditional.
Staging gets two built-in rules but does not get the production-only checks: no fail-closed on a parse error, no policy block. It sits closer to development than the name suggests.
In development and staging, a statement the parser cannot read runs without analysis.
An environment string QoreDB does not recognise falls back to development, silently.
The rate limit is 60 queries of burst per connection, refilling at 6 per second. It stops a runaway loop; it is not tunable and it resets when the app restarts.

The audit trail keeps the most recent 10,000 entries by default and drops the oldest beyond that, without archiving. It is a plain local file: readable, editable and deletable by anything running as you. It is a record for you, not tamper-evident evidence for someone else.
Redaction replaces quoted string literals wholesale, plus known credential shapes. Numbers are not redacted: a value like an account number in a WHERE clause stays legible in the log.

cta.title: The database does not know you meant staging
cta.body: Production guardrails ship in the free QoreDB core. Custom safety rules are Pro.

plugins.title: Plugins
plugins.subtitle: Extend QoreDB with plugins that run as sandboxed WebAssembly, with capabilities you grant one by one and can revoke without a restart.
plugins.teaser: WebAssembly modules with metered fuel, capped memory and bounded time — plus purely declarative plugins that execute no code at all.

problem.title: The problem
problem.body: A database client cannot ship every workflow your team has. The usual answer is a plugin API, and the usual cost is that a plugin becomes code with your privileges: your connections, your credentials, your filesystem. You install one to pretty-print a JSON column and it can read everything you ever query. That is why most people never install any.

solution.title: How QoreDB solves it
solution.intro: QoreDB plugins come in two shapes. The simple ones are pure data: a manifest contributing snippets, connection templates or themes, with no code to run. The rest compile to WebAssembly and execute inside an interpreter with no ambient authority — nothing is reachable unless the manifest asks for it and you grant it.

steps.title: A manifest, and optionally a module
steps.body: Every plugin is a folder with a plugin.json. It can contribute snippets, connection templates, themes, result viewers and commands. If it stops there, no code runs and there is nothing to sandbox. If it ships a .wasm module, the manifest declares which hooks it implements and which capabilities it needs.

steps.title: Seven capabilities, granted individually
steps.body: Log, notify, storage, read query results, HTTP, filesystem, secrets. Every one defaults to off. What you grant is intersected with what the manifest asked for, so editing the consent file by hand cannot widen a plugin's reach beyond its own declaration. Checks happen at call time, which means revoking takes effect immediately rather than at the next restart.

steps.title: Bounded, not trusted
steps.body: Each invocation gets a fresh store, 50 million units of fuel and 16 MB of memory. A hook on the query path has 500 ms; background work gets 5 seconds and a command gets 10. A module that fails three times in a row is unloaded. There is no way to pin the app: the budget runs out first.

steps.title: The network is an allow-list
steps.body: A plugin that wants HTTP names the exact hosts it will call, in its manifest, with no wildcards. Requests resolve DNS first and refuse loopback, private ranges and cloud metadata addresses unless the manifest explicitly asks for private networks. Secrets live in your OS keychain under a separate service from your database credentials, and a module only reads the names it declared.

steps.title: What a plugin cannot reach
steps.body: It cannot execute SQL — that capability was removed rather than gated. It cannot read your connection credentials or your vault. It cannot alter a query result. It cannot leave its own data folder. There is no filesystem beyond that, no sockets, no subprocesses.

example.title: A concrete example
example.intro: A manifest for a plugin that posts executed mutations to an audit endpoint and contributes one snippet:
example.caption: Everything the plugin may do is on this page, in the file, before you install it. The consent dialog shows you the same list.

limits.title: The limits, stated plainly
limits.intro: A sandbox is a set of specific guarantees, not a general promise. Here is what these ones are and are not:
items: Integrity is checked, authorship is not. There are no signatures. The marketplace verifies that an archive matches the SHA-256 the registry recorded, which proves the bytes were not altered in transit — not who wrote them. Whoever controls the registry controls that digest.
The module hash in a manifest is optional. A plugin without one still installs; the interface labels it unsigned.

Reading query results and making HTTP calls are separate capabilities, but a plugin granted both can send every row you query to its allow-listed host. That is the point of an audit plugin, and it is also the exfiltration path. Grant them together on purpose.
The consent dialog pre-selects everything the manifest requested. The model underneath is opt-in; the dialog is not. Read it rather than confirming it.
A plugin with a query-path hook can refuse any query with a reason of its choosing. It is the strongest thing a plugin can do, and it is granted by installing it, not by a capability.
You can install from any URL, not only the marketplace. There is no host allow-list on installation.
Bounds are per invocation, not per plugin: fuel and memory reset on each call. A plugin cannot hang the app, but it can be called often.
The marketplace review is a human reading a pull request on the registry repository. It is not an automated audit and it is not a security certification.

cta.title: Extend the client, not your attack surface
cta.body: The plugin system, WebAssembly runtime included, is part of the free QoreDB core.
